sentences.text: My name is Sandra Segal.
S-A-N-D-R-A S-E-G-A-L.
People call me Sunny, S-U-N-N-Y.
Sara, S-A-R-A.
No, I have not.
My maiden name is Van Leeuwen.
Capital V-A-N, capital L-E-E-U-W-E-N.
I'm 56 years old.
I was born July 8, 1939.
I was born in London, England.
London, L-O-N-D-O-N.
Well, I have to backtrack for that for a minute.
We were-- my parents got married in Holland.
And they decided to go on their honeymoon to London, England.
And they fell in love with it.
And they ended up staying there two years, where my father was a mashgiach there in a bakery.
He watches to make sure that the food stays kosher, what comes in kosher and stays kosher.
Then in 1939 after I was born, my mother's sister was getting married in Holland.
And my father had lost his job, because the war was breaking out.
And they were anti-Jewish, and they didn't want him there.
They told him to go back to Holland.
So they went to the wedding.
And the war broke out there, and we were stuck.
My father's name was Izaak, I-Z-A-A-K.
And his English name [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] is-- his Hebrew name is Yitzhak.
He didn't have an English name.
His Dutch name was Izaak.
And we just called him Yitzhak.
My mother, her name was Yanka Rozalia.
Y-A-N-K-A R-O-Z-A-L-I-A.
And her maiden name was Stahl, S-T-A-H-L.
That is correct.
We went back to Holland.
We were stuck there.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- as-- as my mother tells me, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my uncle had a place to go hiding.
And he begged my-- my mother's mother, who was staying with us because my grandfather was in New York making money to bring the rest of the family over.
And he said, come in hiding with us.
So he wanted just my mother and-- and my brother and myself and my father.
So-- no, I'm sorry.
That's not true.
He asked my mother's mother to go in hiding.
And she says, without everybody else, I can't go, so that we were stuck.
And he went into hiding.
He became Marcel instead of Moshe Stahl.
My brother's a year and a half younger than I am.
His name is Rabbi Levi, L-E-V-I, Van Leeuwen.
And he lives in Kiryat Arba, in Israel.
Not at that time.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My baby brother was born six weeks after the war.
After the war, my father begged my mother.
He says, I was one of 13 children, and they're all gone.
I really would love it if you'd let us have another baby.
So two years after we were freed, she gave birth to a baby boy.
And when my brother [INAUDIBLE] was born-- after he was-- he was six weeks old at the time when my father died.
I want to go back to the pre-war.
And we'll talk more about that part later.
Do you remember much of your life before the war?
Before the war?

No, only the fact that we were coming down when the Nazis-- when the Dutch spies turned us over to the Nazis, I remember coming down the hallway from the top door and asking my mother, what's going to happen to us?
We have to eat kosher.
How are we going to-- how are we going to survive?
And she said that the rabbi had said that we could eat anything to survive.
I don't remember the house.
But we did live on 65 [NON-ENGLISH].
In [INAUDIBLE].
No.
I-- I have a picture somewhere-- I should have made it ready-- of my neighbor across the street that we used to play with.
But I don't-- I don't remember her name right now.
My grandmother, I remember my grandmother only in the fact that I was sitting in the highchair.
My mother says I was about two years old.
I was sucking on a chicken bone, and she was feeding me chicken soup.
That's the only time that I remember her being.
I don't remember her face.
She died in the camps.
When we were taken to the concentration camps.
Not that I remember.
Well, that--
We were hiding.
And that's when the Dutch spies-- as I just said, the Dutch spies turned us over to the Nazis.
I don't know, nothing.
All I remember is that we were upstairs.
And it was a long, down staircase, and we had to go, being herded out the door onto-- onto what, I don't know.
But then we went on to the trains.
My-- my mother for sure and my little brother.
And my father must've been, too.
But I only remember seeing my mother.
Correct, yes.
No.
That my mother told me.
From that particular moment in time, that's all I remember was going down those stairs.
Yeah, how are we going to survive if we have to eat kosher?
We-- we can't eat just anything.
We're going to die, because we can't eat kosher.
So that's when she told me you could-- that the rabbi said you could eat anything, even pig, as long as you keep yourself alive.
You must.
And we were going to Bergen-Belsen.
Bergen-Belsen was a holding camp-- well, let me backtrack for a minute.
First, let me say that, as far as we know now, I am the youngest survivor to come out of the concentration camps in the whole Midwest United States.
Hitler had some kind of a treaty with England and with Switzerland.
Being that I was a firstborn child, and I was a British subject, Hitler put us in this holding camp, like a prisoner of war exchange.
So the camp was divided in two with a fence.
Anne Frank was on the other side.
I found that out later from Anne Frank's girlfriend.
And we were on this side.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] even though Hitler had the-- the treaty, it didn't stop him from at will just taking people away and-- and killing them.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Twice, my brother saved us from the gas chambers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Once he had a terrible ear infection.
And he had a high fever.
And we were supposed to go on a transport to Auschwitz.
And the doctor convinced the Nazis that he's untransferable.
And the doctor was able to give him some morphine to calm him down, so he wouldn't be shot.
He was only a year and a half or maybe two at that time already.
And the second time, again, they wanted to clean us out and bring us to Auschwitz.
And my brother had terrible dysentery.

It was so bad that the doctor said the only thing that's going to save him is if you beg the old German Jews in the camp in the kitchen to save a little bit of rice water to give to him a teaspoon at a time.
That's the only thing that will save him.
So my mother went and she cried and she cried until they gave her some.
And that's how my brother got a little bit better.
And we were still stuck in the camp.
What else do you remember about Bergen-Belsen?
I remember-- what I remember-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember my father coming to visit sometimes in the evenings.
And he had to be-- all the men had to be counted on the way back out.
And they would-- I don't know if it was a rubber hose or a steel hose.
But they would smack the men on the back real hard to count them.
And I remember my father ducked.
And I remember thinking, oh, my god, they're going to kill him.
But they-- they-- the guy just let him go.
I also remember my brother and I couldn't play.
So for many hours we had to shuckle back and forth like this, hours on end.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when the Germans came around at night to make sure that we were going to sleep, he was hitting everybody by the feet real hard.
And I-- I reflexed, I mean, my feet came up, so I wouldn't get hurt.
And then I hid myself under the covers thinking he's going to kill me.
But he didn't do anything.
And I also remember we were in many bunk beds.
Not like American bunk beds where you only have two, there were more.
And there was an old lady on the top bunk bed with a little baby.
And she couldn't come down fast enough when we were called to go to appel.
I will explain to you appel in a minute.
But they were beating her, and the baby was knocked off the bed.
And I remember looking at the baby down at the ground, and the baby was dismembered.
Then we had to go stand on appel.
Now, it didn't matter if it was freezing winter or if it was boiling hot in the summer, we had to stand dead still like this to be counted.
I don't know if there were hundreds of us or thousands of us, we had to stand there and be counted.
If anybody sneezed, they started all over again.
Somebody coughed, started all over again.
And I remember tugging on my mother skirt and telling her I had to go to the bathroom.
And she said, shh.
And I don't know what I did, but we had to stand there and be counted all over again.
Now, in my mind, I remember standing naked with everybody.
But I think my mother said that we were not naked, just that we were cold, and we were wearing almost nothing.
We were able to-- to run to the-- to camps-- when they got us, we were able to take a little bundle of stuff.
And when we outgrew that, my parents would barter stuff.
When the French people came to the camps, they were not used to the hunger yet.
And they were well-fed.
And they had nice clothes.
They just-- French style.
They knew what to buy.
They had good stuff on them.
So my father and my mother each would save three days' ration of bread and give it to them for a little sweater for my-- outfit for my brother.
Or a sweater for my mother, my father saved his bread.
And so that way they got clothing back and forth.
It was my mother's job to serve-- bring the soup-- quote, 'soup'.
It was water in which kohlrabi was cooked.
And we got the water.

And she was in a chain gang tied together with other women with buckets of soup that she had to bring-- bring the water back and forth-- bring the water from wherever they got the water and brought it to the kitchen to cook.
Also, my mother, even though she had terrible problem with her leg-- it was completely swollen with edema.
She was in a lot of pain.
She could hardly stand up.
Now, imagine, she's hungry.
She's getting thinner.
She's doing the soup chain thing with her leg.
They came at inspection time when everybody was making their beds.
We were inspected to see how well we made it.
And the Nazi came in, and said, to whom does this bed belong?
So my mother says, you stupid idiot, you had to make it good?
It was perfect.
So the Nazi made an example out of her and said, from now on, you will make all 260 beds on top of your other work.
And she had to do that also.
She was an agony, so she had those two jobs.
And then she would save-- whatever little soup we did get, sometimes she would use that soup to shampoo us and wash us down.
Or, she'd use it to wash out some clothing we had.
No.
That's it.
The Red Cross, though, didn't think so.
When the-- the-- I guess the world was getting concerned.
And they were sending in the Red Cross.
The Red Cross always came-- and I don't know why-- announced.
So, all right.
They knew they were coming.
They took the latest newcomers to the camps and showed how nice and rounded out they where.
And they put a lot of ham and, I guess, vegetables, whatever.
They put some stuff in the soup.
And they said, see?
They're treated pretty nicely.
We're doing OK here.
And that's what the Red Cross came back with.
So my mother remembered saying to herself that, oh good, now we get to eat something today.
Soon as the Red Cross left, they fished out everything from that water.
And we got the soup, nothing in it.
Yes.
I remember other children in the barracks.
But I don't remember if they were near my bunk beds.
No.
That's--
No.
That's all we were allowed to do.
We were-- we couldn't-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that's all we could do.
I think my mother said every night at 8 o'clock.
They kept the men and women separate.
And they kept the Jews separate from the non-Jews that were in the concentration camps.
It wasn't just Jews that were taken.
Yes.
I saw him pushing a wheelbarrow on the other side of the fence.
But I don't know what he did for-- for the Nazis there.
He never spoke about it after the war.
I was three when I went in, and five and a half when I came out.
No.
Bergen-Belsen was a holding camp.
And from time to time, they would ship us out to either Amersfoort or-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I've forgotten the name of the other camp.
But it'll come back to me in a minute.
What happened was, they couldn't fit all the people to be gassed into the camps.
So they would put them into Bergen-Belsen also, and then move them on to the trains further.
And in order to make room for them, we had to clear out for a while.
And then they'd bring us back-- Westerbork.
Westerbork and Amersfoort were the two camps we were shipped back and forth, a month here or two months there.
Amersfoort was in Holland.
That my mother told me.
I don't-- I didn't remember that.
No, I do not.
I don't remember.
No, I don't remember being transported.
All I remember was Bergen-Belsen.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember-- now, I don't know if this was in the camps, but I don't know where else it could've been-- sitting on the toilets.
There were toilets all in a row with no doors.
And I remember hiding my head in shame, hoping nobody would see, like a little kid would putting your-- in like a stork, put your head in the sand.
And nobody will know you're there.
So it had to have been there.
I don't know where else it would've been.
I would think-- I don't know, I would think that being that I survived it that my health was pretty good, even though I was tremendously malnourished.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My brother to this day is near blind, because of the malnourishment.
My mother's health was fine except for the fact that she had terrible ulcers right after the war.
No.
And my father died of a heart attack at 38, two years after liberation.
I remember at the end of the war-- I have to backtrack before the soldiers.

At the end of the war-- towards the end of the war, when the Nazis heard that the Russians and the British were coming from either end to liberate the camp, they wanted to try and clear all of us out.
So they started pushing people on the trains to go be gassed.
And they instructed the men to pile up the dead bodies and half-dead bodies like logs and make-- what do you call them, pyres?
Flames?
No.
Like-- like a bonfire, a human bonfire.
And while the Nazis were busy loading up the people on the train, some of the men decided that they're going to eat the livers of the people they were burning.
Because that was the first food that they-- who knew what was going to be?
You know, you're going to be going on the train to be gassed, so here was a chance to eat something.
So we were-- it was our turn to go on the trains to be gassed.
And miracle of miracles, there were bombs overhead.
And the-- the Nazis came running out of the train.
And when they saw that the Nazis were running, they stopped bombing.
And we were-- some people were screaming that we should head for the hills, which were right around the tracks, to hide our faces in the sand and lay flat against the sand hills.
So that in case the bombs do come, either they would miss us, or I don't know for what reason they told us to do it.
But we did.

And my mother said that we tried to make-- I don't remember if she said from potatoes or potato starch that the Nazis left on the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] trains-- that they tried to make fires and tried to cook that.
But the last Nazis, as they were running off the plane, kicked over everything, so we couldn't eat it.
Then we went to-- I don't know which soldiers came to help us.
But we were sent to a little village called Trobitz.
And the people there were crying.
They never knew these things happened.
They knew from nothing.
I say, how could they not know?
With all the stench and all the stuff that's going on and the people talking in the towns, they had to know.
Were they going to say, I knew, and I didn't do something about it?
Of course, they knew.
So the soldiers forced the little villagers to take us in and to feed us.
We were on a little chicken farm.
And the one soldier truck came with bread.
And all of us, a lot of people, ran out to get the bread.
And my mother sent me.
But I was a little shrimp.
And all these people were huge.
And all-- I was sticking my hand up to get the loaf.
And all the other people were overhead grabbing for the bread.
And I remember the soldier saying, this little girl was here first.
And I took the bread.
And I went inside with that bread.
And then the--
Well, that was-- in the beginning, not.
They fed us baby food.
Then the soldiers said-- the American soldier said, you must eat a half a teaspoon of baby food at a time.
We will feed you.
You will get, don't worry.
Some people didn't listen.
And they ate, and they ate.
And they gobbled everything up that they could get their hands on, a chocolate bar.
Whatever they found that the soldiers had, they wanted it.
And they didn't listen, and they died.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The-- the Russian soldiers also came to the little village, came to our house where we were staying and raped the grandmother and the mother and a 16-year-old girl.
The father was one of the Nazis in the War.
I don't know where my father was at the time.
But the Russians came upstairs to us and took my mother around the waist and said, the Germans we take by force.
And the Jews we take with love.
Now, he must have spoken a little Yiddish or else my mother wouldn't have been able to understand.
And she said, my god, give me-- give me a sign.
How am I going to get out of this?
So she screamed, typhus typhus!
And the guy was so scared of getting typhoid fever that he ran down the stairs.
He almost broke his neck.
She never saw him again.
Do you remember this?
This my mother told me.
This was in Trobitz.
When?
Somewhere between May and April.
I remember being in army trucks, hoarded in army trucks.
But I don't know if that was to get to Bergen-Belsen, or if that was to get to the train, or if that was to get from Trobitz to [INAUDIBLE].
I somehow get the feeling that it was to go to the camps.
But I don't know.
All I remember was it was an open army truck, you know, nothing on top of us.
And we were all squished in there.
I don't remember.
Skinny.
Yes.
All of us had typhoid fever, [INAUDIBLE], and [INAUDIBLE] fever, typhoid fever, very, very bad.
And we had lice.
And they shaved us completely bald.
I guess the nurses.
I don't remember.
After that, they-- the Dutch people gave us a certificate to come back, that we were allowed to come back.
And they gave us a house on the same street-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] on the same side, as a matter of fact-- down the block from where we used to live.
When we came into that house, there was nothing in that house, except on the wall was a picture of Hitler.
And when my brother and I saw that picture of Hitler, we put it on the ground.
And both of us jumped on it.
[CRYING] [SOBBING] All our hatred came out on that stupid picture.
[CRYING]
[SIGHS] [SIGHS]
